concise:
Ayah 1–6 · linked video: 01. Al-Hujurat (Ayah 1-6)

Surah Al-Hujurat: Ayahs 1-6

Ayah 1

يَـٰٓأَيُّهَا ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ لَا تُقَدِّمُوا۟ بَيْنَ يَدَىِ ٱللَّهِ وَرَسُولِهِۦ ۖ وَٱتَّقُوا۟ ٱللَّهَ ۚ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ سَمِيعٌ عَلِيمٌ

After studying Surah Muhammad, which discussed the role and respect we’re supposed to show the Prophet ﷺ, and after journeying through Surah Al-Fath, which was from beginning to end a demonstration of loyalty to the Prophet ﷺ, we now examine Surah Al-Hujurat. This surah also begins with parameters and limits on how we engage with and discuss the Prophet ﷺ. It addresses how serious the matter of love, respect and loyalty for the Prophet ﷺ truly is, and what the consequences are of not taking these matters seriously.

This surah is particularly important because it establishes the principles of an Islamic community. By community, I don’t mean thousands of people in a convention hall. I mean people who visit each other’s homes, your social circle, your extended family, the friends you actually spend time with. How are you supposed to interact with these people? What’s beautiful is how these two subjects connect: respect for the Messenger ﷺ transitions into our respect for each other. When you have deep love for the Prophet ﷺ, that love becomes infectious. Anyone else who loves him automatically earns your love too. It becomes difficult to harbor hatred for anyone who says “Muhammad Rasulullah ﷺ.”

The surah begins: “O you who believe, do not put yourselves ahead of Allah and His Messenger.” Don’t make yourselves the priority before Allah ﷻ and His Messenger ﷺ. When they say something, what you want must take a back seat. The Prophet ﷺ taught us that none of us truly has iman until whatever we desire is placed in submission to whatever revelation came. There will be times when our temptations, wants and desires conflict with revelation. This ayah calls us to never allow ourselves to take priority, to never compromise Allah’s regulations for our own agenda.

The command وَٱتَّقُوا۟ ٱللَّهَ (and be cautious of Allah) reminds us that certainly Allah ﷻ hears everything and knows everything. This ayah concerns the deen of Allah ﷻ, the regulations of Allah ﷻ, never allowing them to take a back seat while our agenda takes priority.

Ayah 2

يَـٰٓأَيُّهَا ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ لَا تَرْفَعُوٓا۟ أَصْوَٰتَكُمْ فَوْقَ صَوْتِ ٱلنَّبِىِّ وَلَا تَجْهَرُوا۟ لَهُۥ بِٱلْقَوْلِ كَجَهْرِ بَعْضِكُمْ لِبَعْضٍ أَن تَحْبَطَ أَعْمَـٰلُكُمْ وَأَنتُمْ لَا تَشْعُرُونَ

The next ayah shifts to respect in our conduct: “O you who believe, don’t raise your voices above the voice of the Prophet ﷺ.” When engaging in conversation with him, a show of respect means your voice should be lower than his. Some people naturally have louder voices, and some cultures simply speak loudly as the norm. The Bedouins were very loud people. But when it comes to the Prophet ﷺ, your culture doesn’t count. You can’t simply say, “This is just how we do things.”

What does this mean for us today, when the Prophet’s ﷺ voice is no longer physically heard? Yes it is, because the Quran is also the Prophet’s voice. It’s the word of Allah ﷻ, but the original voice that humanity heard was the voice of the Prophet ﷺ. The actual hadith of the Prophet ﷺ embody his voice as well.

What does it mean practically not to raise our voice above his? When someone quotes a hadith to you about something you’re doing, don’t immediately argue or debate. If someone tells you, “Brother, there’s a hadith about what you’re doing,” don’t turn it into a contest. Don’t play tennis with the hadith. If you hear someone citing the Prophet ﷺ, whether correctly or incorrectly, and you don’t have better knowledge, just be quiet. This is a matter for the scholars, not for casual debate.

We must play it safe. You don’t want to end up dismissing something the Prophet ﷺ actually said. If there’s even a small chance that what someone is saying contains an element of truth, you don’t want to be on the opposing side of what the Prophet ﷺ said.

The ayah continues: وَلَا تَجْهَرُوا۟ لَهُۥ بِٱلْقَوْلِ كَجَهْرِ بَعْضِكُمْ لِبَعْضٍ “And don’t call out to him as you call out to one another.” Don’t be casual with the Prophet ﷺ.

The historical context reveals that Bedouins new to Islam would come to the Prophet’s apartment and stand outside calling, “Muhammad! Muhammad! Come out, we need to talk to you!” To them, it wasn’t a big deal. The Prophet’s name itself is praiseworthy—Muhammad means “the one who is praised and thanked continually.” But Allah ﷻ and His angels are continually doing salawat upon the Prophet ﷺ. Every human being has at least two angels assigned to them, and all these angels are doing salawat upon the Prophet ﷺ. This is why Allah ﷻ says: إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ وَمَلَـٰٓئِكَتَهُۥ يُصَلُّونَ عَلَى ٱلنَّبِىِّ “Indeed, Allah and His angels send blessings upon the Prophet. O you who believe, send blessings upon him.”

This was a newly forming society in early phases. The Muslim community is like a large family, and the father figure of that family is the Prophet ﷺ. Because it was a new family, they didn’t yet know what it meant to deal with each other as a healthy family. This is community counseling—setting the parameters for how things should go. People must show the utmost respect to the Prophet ﷺ. You will not raise your voice. You will not talk about him casually as you talk about anyone else.

Why is this important and timeless? Because now the Prophet ﷺ is a historical figure, but you will not talk about him like any other historical figure. You will not discuss him as merely a political leader, though he was one. Not as just a teacher, though he was one. Not as simply a friend, though he was one.

Consider this remarkable shift: Abbas (RA) was his uncle, Hamza (RA) was his uncle, Fatima (RA) was his daughter, Aisha (RA) and Khadijah (RA) were his wives, Abu Bakr (RA) was his best friend. These were people with personal, lifelong relationships with him. Fatima (RA) didn’t suddenly become his daughter when he became a Prophet—she always was. Abu Bakr (RA) wasn’t his best friend only after becoming Muslim—he always was. Hamza (RA) didn’t just become his uncle after Islam—he always was.

Yet when they understood this teaching, you don’t find Hamza (RA) saying “my nephew said” or Fatima (RA) saying “my father said” or Abu Bakr (RA) saying “my best friend.” They all said “Qala Rasulullah ﷺ” (the Messenger of Allah said). Understand what kind of mental shift this represents. Yesterday he was my cousin, my nephew, my husband. There’s a certain informality between husband and wife, between cousins, between best friends. But once this revelation came, all those relationships had to be reconfigured.

For us, showing grand respect to the Prophet ﷺ comes more easily because that’s all we know of him—this great, magnificent leader. For them, it was a huge shift, because he was their best friend, uncle, cousin, neighbor. This is why Allah ﷻ says to this new community: understand the implications. Once you say “Rasulullah,” everything changes. You can never talk about him casually again.

The consequences are severe: أَن تَحْبَطَ أَعْمَـٰلُكُمْ وَأَنتُمْ لَا تَشْعُرُونَ “Lest your deeds become worthless while you don’t even realize.” You fought alongside him in battle. You migrated with him from Makkah. You spent all your assets for him. But if you talk about him casually, all your good deeds—whatever jihad, whatever spending, whatever prayers you’ve accomplished—could be nullified, and you won’t even realize what happened. You could stand before Allah ﷻ with no good deeds remaining.

Ayah 3

إِنَّ ٱلَّذِينَ يَغُضُّونَ أَصْوَٰتَهُمْ عِندَ رَسُولِ ٱللَّهِ أُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ ٱلَّذِينَ ٱمْتَحَنَ ٱللَّهُ قُلُوبَهُمْ لِلتَّقْوَىٰ ۚ لَهُم مَّغْفِرَةٌ وَأَجْرٌ عَظِيمٌ

This ayah presents the beautiful contrast: “Indeed, those who lower their voices in the presence of the Messenger of Allah ﷺ—those are the ones whose hearts Allah has tested for taqwa.”

The taqwa mentioned here specifically refers to respect for the Messenger ﷺ. Allah ﷻ tested their taqwa by having them lower their voices before the Prophet ﷺ. They understood: this is the Messenger of Allah ﷻ. He speaks on behalf of Allah ﷻ, so showing him respect is, by extension, showing Allah ﷻ respect. For such people, there is forgiveness and tremendous reward.

Ayah 4

إِنَّ ٱلَّذِينَ يُنَادُونَكَ مِن وَرَآءِ ٱلْحُجُرَٰتِ أَكْثَرُهُمْ لَا يَعْقِلُونَ

“Indeed, those who call you from behind the apartments—most of them don’t understand.”

Notice the merciful phrasing here. There’s a difference between saying “you don’t know” and “you’re ignorant.” Which is more respectful, more merciful? Allah ﷻ doesn’t say أَكْثَرُهُمْ جَاهِلُونَ (most of them are ignorant). He says أَكْثَرُهُمْ لَا يَعْقِلُونَ (most of them don’t understand). They’re swayed by emotion, not thinking things through. The word عَقْل means to hold yourself back, to use restraint. It means you can’t just say whatever comes to your mouth or do whatever you feel like doing. Most of them lack this restraint.

Ayah 5

وَلَوْ أَنَّهُمْ صَبَرُوا۟ حَتَّىٰ تَخْرُجَ إِلَيْهِمْ لَكَانَ خَيْرًا لَّهُمْ ۚ وَٱللَّهُ غَفُورٌ رَّحِيمٌ

“And if only they had been patient until you came out to them, it would have been better for them. And Allah is Forgiving and Merciful.”

This is showing loving mercy. While their behavior was a serious matter—showing impatience with the Prophet ﷺ—Allah ﷻ is letting it go this time. وَٱللَّهُ غَفُورٌ رَّحِيمٌ “Allah is Forgiving and Merciful.”

Ayah 6

يَـٰٓأَيُّهَا ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوٓا۟ إِن جَآءَكُمْ فَاسِقٌۢ بِنَبَإٍ فَتَبَيَّنُوٓا۟ أَن تُصِيبُوا۟ قَوْمًۢا بِجَهَـٰلَةٍ فَتُصْبِحُوا۟ عَلَىٰ مَا فَعَلْتُمْ نَـٰدِمِينَ

Now the surah shifts to Muslims dealing with each other: “O you who believe, if a corrupt person comes to you with news, then verify it.”

The word فَاسِق refers to a corrupt person. What makes someone corrupt in this context? They hear something and immediately pass it along without verification. The ayah commands us to investigate and make sure we understand what’s actually happening before acting on information from such a person.

Ayah 6–11 · linked video: 02. Al-Hujurat (Ayah 6-11)

Surah Al-Hujurat: Ayahs 6-11

Ayah 6

يَـٰٓأَيُّهَا ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوٓا۟ إِن جَآءَكُمْ فَاسِقٌۢ بِنَبَإٍ فَتَبَيَّنُوٓا۟ أَن تُصِيبُوا۟ قَوْمًۢا بِجَهَـٰلَةٍ فَتُصْبِحُوا۟ عَلَىٰ مَا فَعَلْتُمْ نَـٰدِمِينَ

The Corruption of Information

Consider how information deteriorates as it passes from person to person. Bob pushes Joe and Joe falls down. The first person whispers this to the second, who whispers to the third, and so on through forty people. By the time you ask the fortieth person what they heard, the story has transformed completely: “Bob ran Joe over with his car, then burned his house down, and I can’t even begin to tell you what he did with his family.” Each person added just 0.1% of their own interpretation, but multiply that tiny addition across forty people and suddenly you have a 40% distortion. This is فِسْق - corruption in action.

The psychological reality of communication is complex. What the speaker intends and what the listener understands can be two entirely different things. What I actually said and what you thought I said are often not the same. Then when you communicate what you think I said to someone else, they hear it through their own filter. This continuous process of filtering through our minds creates layers of distortion.

The Command to Verify

When news comes to you from a corrupt source - a فَاسِق , someone who is corrupt - you must verify absolutely everything. Allah ﷻ commands فَتَبَيَّنُوٓا۟ - investigate thoroughly. Everything should be separated and examined piece by piece. This happened? What’s the proof? That happened? What’s the proof? Get clarity on every single detail. If there’s no proof, then don’t talk about it.

This plays out in our communities constantly. “Did you hear that the imam is going to quit his job? He’s going to leave the masjid.” You respond, “Really? Where is he going?” And they reply, “From what I hear, he’s moving out of town. The board doesn’t like him.” Now you attend another Eid gathering and someone asks about the fundraiser at that masjid, and you share what you “heard.” This is fisq - spreading unverified information.

The Prophet ﷺ taught us that Allah ﷻ hates three things for us, and one of them is قِيلَ وَقَال - passing along what you merely heard without verification. Leave that stuff alone entirely.

The Harm of Casual Gossip

Allah ﷻ warns: أَن تُصِيبُوا۟ قَوْمًۢا بِجَهَـٰلَةٍ - that you might afflict or target a nation, a group of people, ignorantly. The word جَهَـٰلَةٍ here carries the meaning of acting on impulse, even oppression. For you it might be casual conversation over chai, but for someone else it could destroy their reputation and cause immense harm.

I’ve witnessed this pattern repeatedly in organizations and among students. Someone has a problem, and instead of going directly to the person they have the issue with, they talk to someone else, then someone else, then someone else. That original problem was tiny, but by the time the whole community hears about it, it has snowballed into a mountain. Everyone is angry and upset, and people are demanding that “something needs to be done about this.” Done about what? The original issue was small. But we magnified it out of proportion through careless talk.

Keep it a small issue and it will stay small. Make a big deal out of it through gossip and speculation, and it will become a massive problem. فَتُصْبِحُوا۟ عَلَىٰ مَا فَعَلْتُمْ نَـٰدِمِينَ - you will be filled with shame and regret over what you committed thoughtlessly, over what you did without even realizing the consequences.

Ayah 7

وَٱعْلَمُوٓا۟ أَنَّ فِيكُمْ رَسُولَ ٱللَّهِ ۚ لَوْ يُطِيعُكُمْ فِى كَثِيرٍ مِّنَ ٱلْأَمْرِ لَعَنِتُّمْ وَلَـٰكِنَّ ٱللَّهَ حَبَّبَ إِلَيْكُمُ ٱلْإِيمَـٰنَ وَزَيَّنَهُۥ فِى قُلُوبِكُمْ وَكَرَّهَ إِلَيْكُمُ ٱلْكُفْرَ وَٱلْفُسُوقَ وَٱلْعِصْيَانَ ۚ أُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ هُمُ ٱلرَّٰشِدُونَ

Recognizing True Leadership

وَٱعْلَمُوٓا۟ أَنَّ فِيكُمْ رَسُولَ ٱللَّهِ - you had better know that in your midst is the Messenger of Allah ﷺ. And we can extend this today: you had better know that in your midst is the legacy of the Messenger of Allah ﷺ.

لَوْ يُطِيعُكُمْ فِى كَثِيرٍ مِّنَ ٱلْأَمْرِ لَعَنِتُّمْ - if he started obeying you in most decision-making, all of you would be harmed. Consider the Battle of Uhud. The Prophet ﷺ had taken suggestions before about military strategy. Should we go out and fight the enemy, or should we stay within the confines of Madinah? Some of the younger Companions, particularly those who had accepted Islam after Badr, were enthusiastic: “We want to go out to the battlefield and fight them! We don’t want to stay in the city.” They were eager for martyrdom, excited for battle.

The Prophet’s ﷺ own suggestion was that they should stay in the city. But he went with their preference, and things went badly. Allah ﷻ revealed about that moment: “You were the ones wishing for death, and then when battle started, you were staring at it, stunned.” That was heavy. If the Prophet ﷺ starts following the community’s opinions in most matters, harm would come to everyone.

The Gift of Beautified Iman

وَلَـٰكِنَّ ٱللَّهَ حَبَّبَ إِلَيْكُمُ ٱلْإِيمَـٰنَ - however, Allah ﷻ has made iman beloved to you and beautified it in your hearts. Notice how iman is connected to loyalty and love for the Messenger ﷺ throughout this surah. Earlier, respect for Allah ﷻ was tied to respect for the Messenger ﷺ. Now, if the Prophet ﷺ starts taking too much influence from the community’s opinions, everyone would be harmed. But Allah ﷻ has beautified iman in your hearts.

Which iman is being discussed here? While iman encompasses belief in Allah ﷻ, the Hereafter, the angels, the books and divine decree, the heart of it in this context is iman in the Messenger of Allah ﷺ. Allah ﷻ has beautified this in your hearts.

وَكَرَّهَ إِلَيْكُمُ ٱلْكُفْرَ وَٱلْفُسُوقَ وَٱلْعِصْيَانَ - and He has made disbelief disgusting to you, and on top of that, corruption (fusuq), and on top of that, any disobedience. You hate these things. أُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ هُمُ ٱلرَّٰشِدُونَ - those are the people who are truly headed in the right direction.

Ayah 8

فَضْلًا مِّنَ ٱللَّهِ وَنِعْمَةً ۚ وَٱللَّهُ عَلِيمٌ حَكِيمٌ

فَضْلًا مِّنَ ٱللَّهِ وَنِعْمَةً - this is a huge favor from Allah ﷻ and a luxury, an ease that has been given to you. وَٱللَّهُ عَلِيمٌ حَكِيمٌ - and Allah ﷻ is All-Knowing, full of wisdom.

Ayah 9

وَإِن طَآئِفَتَانِ مِنَ ٱلْمُؤْمِنِينَ ٱقْتَتَلُوا۟ فَأَصْلِحُوا۟ بَيْنَهُمَا ۖ فَإِنۢ بَغَتْ إِحْدَىٰهُمَا عَلَى ٱلْأُخْرَىٰ فَقَـٰتِلُوا۟ ٱلَّتِى تَبْغِى حَتَّىٰ تَفِىٓءَ إِلَىٰٓ أَمْرِ ٱللَّهِ ۚ فَإِن فَآءَتْ فَأَصْلِحُوا۟ بَيْنَهُمَا بِٱلْعَدْلِ وَأَقْسِطُوٓا۟ ۖ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ يُحِبُّ ٱلْمُقْسِطِينَ

When Believers Fight

Sometimes Muslims violate the rights of the Prophet ﷺ by speaking too loudly in his presence. Similarly, Muslims can end up violating each other’s rights, and there’s a progression to this. If you speak badly about one another, if you allow unverified news to circulate and escalate, it can deteriorate into physical fighting. This is why fighting is mentioned here as a progression of bad communication.

وَإِن طَآئِفَتَانِ مِنَ ٱلْمُؤْمِنِينَ ٱقْتَتَلُوا۟ - if two groups from among those who truly believe fall into fighting. Notice that Allah ﷻ calls both groups مُؤْمِنِينَ - true believers, not just muslimīn. Even when two Muslim groups are fighting each other, you cannot say, “These are the believers and those are the hypocrites.” Allah ﷻ calls both of them believers.

The verb ٱقْتَتَلُوا۟ is profound. It doesn’t simply mean they’re fighting each other - it literally means they’re killing themselves. Fighting another Muslim is fighting yourself. The Prophet ﷺ taught that a Muslim is like a mirror to another Muslim. The Ummah is described as one body. If you’re fighting one part of it, you’re using one knife to cut your other hand. This is suicidal behavior. Allah ﷻ expresses His disgust at this reality through this choice of words.

The Process of Reconciliation

Let’s understand this ayah as a divine equation with multiple groups. Group A and Group B are fighting each other. Allah ﷻ says فَأَصْلِحُوا۟ بَيْنَهُمَا - make peace between both of them. This command is directed at Group C, the neutral party, the rest of the community that isn’t involved in the fight.

Imagine brothers in one class and sisters in another class are fighting each other, throwing things at one another. People from a neighboring masjid come over and say, “Calm down, everyone. What’s going on here?” Things settle down. You bring both parties to the table for negotiations. “Why are you fighting?” “They keep taking our ping-pong table!” All the issues are discussed, everyone agrees, and they acknowledge, “We’re Muslims, we shouldn’t behave this way. We’re sorry.” All problems are resolved, peace is made.

When Peace is Violated

But then the sisters decide they want trouble again - and yes, I usually pick on the brothers, so it’s only fair to mention the sisters sometimes too. They come to campus and discover their ping-pong table is missing. The fight starts again.

فَإِنۢ بَغَتْ إِحْدَىٰهُمَا عَلَى ٱلْأُخْرَىٰ - if one of them rebels against the other. Notice: peace has already been made. The neutral party made that peace. Now the fight starts again with one group initiating active aggression.

فَقَـٰتِلُوا۟ ٱلَّتِى تَبْغِى - fight the one who rebelled. Everyone must unite against the aggressor. If Group B violated the treaty, then Group A and Group C must both stand against Group B.

Here’s something crucial to understand: when you fight someone, you naturally develop a grudge against them. Originally, Group A was angry at Group B. But now both Group A and Group C are angry at Group B. Group C was supposed to be the neutral arbiter, the negotiator. But they’re no longer neutral because they’re angry - they just had to fight Group B. They’re thinking, “I was the one who made the peace treaty between both of you. You spit on that treaty I made. You have no respect for the negotiations I worked so hard on. I don’t like these guys anymore.”

Justice After Reconciliation

حَتَّىٰ تَفِىٓءَ إِلَىٰٓ أَمْرِ ٱللَّهِ - until they return to the command of Allah ﷻ. The word تَفِىٓءَ means to return from a bad state to a good state. And notice what Allah ﷻ calls the peace treaty: أَمْرِ ٱللَّهِ - the command of Allah ﷻ. This is incredible. When Muslims make peace with each other, that peace itself is the command of Allah ﷻ. Violating that peace is violating the command of Allah ﷻ.

فَإِن فَآءَتْ - and if the rebellious group does come back, saying “Okay, fine, we’re ready to negotiate again,” what happens? فَأَصْلِحُوا۟ بَيْنَهُمَا بِٱلْعَدْلِ - make peace between them with justice, with adl.

This is the second time in this ayah we’re reading the command to make peace between the two groups, but this time there’s a crucial difference. The first time, no specific manner was mentioned. This time, Allah ﷻ adds بِٱلْعَدْلِ - with justice. Why?

Open and Transparent Justice

The negotiating party is now biased because they’re angry. They had to fight Group B, so they harbor resentment. This is where the concept of عَدْل becomes essential. There are two important words for justice in Arabic: قِسْط and عَدْل. قِسْط means to make something half and half - you get half, I get half. It’s about equal distribution. But عَدْل is deeper - it comes from the root meaning “open, public, transparent justice.”

Consider what happened: the first time these Muslim groups fought, concerned community members brought both parties to their home privately. They had dinner together, broke bread, prayed together, and said, “Look, we’re brothers in Islam. We’re supposed to represent our deen. If we’re fighting each other, this will create a scandal. People will say Muslims only fight each other. We shouldn’t do this.” So they made peace privately, which was appropriate - keeping the dirty laundry internal.

But then one of those sides went out afterwards and created trouble, claiming, “You don’t know what happened. We went to their house and they made a peace treaty, but it was so messed up. We didn’t even get to speak.” Now you’ve fought them, and they’re being brought back to justice. You can’t trust a closed-door meeting anymore. They’ll just go back and make trouble again.

This time, even though it’s humiliating to bring out the conflicts of Muslims into public view, it has to be عَدْل - open, transparent, public justice. The next time they claim, “That meeting was entirely unfair,” you can respond, “What are you talking about? It’s on YouTube. Everyone knows what you said. It’s out in the open now.”

Justice in All Aspects

وَأَقْسِطُوٓا۟ - and be fair in your personal dealings. This is both public and private justice. عَدْل applies to the formal court system and dispute management, which is why the Messenger ﷺ was commanded to say, “I have been commanded to do open justice between you because there’s obviously a conflict here.”

But قِسْط applies to your private life, your personal interactions. You should be committed to fairness everywhere: pay your employees full wages, show up to work on time, don’t cheat your boss at work, don’t cut classes if your parents are paying your college tuition, don’t deny your wife her mahr even if you haven’t saved up yet, don’t shortchange your daughter in her share of the inheritance. These are the kinds of everyday fairness that قِسْط encompasses.

إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ يُحِبُّ ٱلْمُقْسِطِينَ - no doubt Allah ﷻ loves those who maintain justice. If the Muslim community is going to survive, the first thing is love of the Prophet ﷺ - that will bind you together. But if there is no justice in the society, then religious values become meaningless. People end up fighting and killing each other despite shared beliefs.

What’s the biggest problem in the Muslim world today? Economic injustice, political injustice, social injustice, class injustice, racial injustice, tribal injustice. We are absolutely marred by injustice in every form.

Ayah 10

إِنَّمَا ٱلْمُؤْمِنُونَ إِخْوَةٌ فَأَصْلِحُوا۟ بَيْنَ أَخَوَيْكُمْ ۚ وَٱتَّقُوا۟ ٱللَّهَ لَعَلَّكُمْ تُرْحَمُونَ

إِنَّمَا ٱلْمُؤْمِنُونَ إِخْوَةٌ - believers are truly nothing other than brothers. You know, when a group of brothers forms a fraternity, they call it إِخْوَان - a brotherhood, a confident group of brothers. But in this ayah, Allah ﷻ says no - true believers are your actual brothers and sisters, not metaphorically. This relationship is as real as the bond of blood.

This is beautifully placed here because actual siblings fight. When you say “brother” casually - “Hey brother, how’s it going?” - you don’t necessarily fight. But your own younger brother? “Get out of my room!” You fight with your siblings constantly. Allah ﷻ is saying, “Look, you’re now brothers to each other in faith. You’re in each other’s lives, you’re very close to each other, so fighting is natural. It’s going to happen.”

فَأَصْلِحُوا۟ بَيْنَ أَخَوَيْكُمْ - when it happens, make peace between your two brothers. You’re the third brother, and those two are fighting - make sure you make peace between them. This is a command of Allah ﷻ.

Don’t think this doesn’t apply inside your home. When your children are fighting each other - “I hate my older sister, she’s always taking my stuff!” - that’s happening in Muslim families constantly. It’s not just their problem to work out. Allah ﷻ commands you to make peace between them. And if you can’t make peace inside the house between your own children, how will you make peace outside the house in the community?

وَٱتَّقُوا۟ ٱللَّهَ لَعَلَّكُمْ تُرْحَمُونَ - be cautious of Allah ﷻ, maintain consciousness of Him, so that all of you may be shown mercy.

Ayah 11

يَـٰٓأَيُّهَا ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ لَا يَسْخَرْ قَوْمٌ مِّن قَوْمٍ عَسَىٰٓ أَن يَكُونُوا۟ خَيْرًا مِّنْهُمْ وَلَا نِسَآءٌ مِّن نِّسَآءٍ عَسَىٰٓ أَن يَكُنَّ خَيْرًا مِّنْهُنَّ ۖ وَلَا تَلْمِزُوٓا۟ أَنفُسَكُمْ وَلَا تَنَابَزُوا۟ بِٱلْأَلْقَـٰبِ ۖ بِئْسَ ٱلِٱسْمُ ٱلْفُسُوقُ بَعْدَ ٱلْإِيمَـٰنِ ۚ وَمَن لَّمْ يَتُبْ فَأُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ هُمُ ٱلظَّـٰلِمُونَ

The Disease of Mockery

يَـٰٓأَيُّهَا ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ لَا يَسْخَرْ قَوْمٌ مِّن قَوْمٍ - O you who claim to believe, don’t let one group of you make fun of another group. Notice that Allah ﷻ didn’t say one individual shouldn’t make fun of another individual. Why? Because people tend to be most obnoxious and mocking when they’re in groups, not alone.

When do people really get into making fun of others? When they’re together with others. The collective intelligence of people - particularly youth - drops when they’re in groups. Individually they might be fine, but put certain people together and the intellect just plummets. There are people who make you dumber just by being around them.

A friend is someone who is true to you, not just a yes-man who always agrees with everything you say. I once had a friend like this - I loved this guy. He had a sharp sense of humor, and when we got together, we would say the meanest things, constantly cracking jokes. My wife told me, “Whenever he’s around, you become a really bad person. Something happens to you.” It was a phenomenon. A group of people get together and they’re just making jokes constantly, always at someone’s expense.

The Scope of Mockery

سُخْرِيَة means to put somebody down in any way. You’re putting down their physical ability, commenting on how fat they are, how skinny they are, how smart or not smart they are, what their major was, where they live, what they do for a living, their accent, their glasses, their home, their neighborhood, their car, their computer, their phone. You just find something - anything - to make fun of. Don’t do that. Don’t put people down like that.

وَلَا نِسَآءٌ مِّن نِّسَآءٍ - and women should not mock other women. The ayah specifically mentions both men and women separately, acknowledging that this happens in both genders.

عَسَىٰٓ أَن يَكُونُوا۟ خَيْرًا مِّنْهُمْ - perhaps they are better than them. The person you’re mocking might actually be better than you in Allah’s sight.

Beyond Surface-Level Humor

Healthy humor is one thing. But the people whose feelings are hurt by mockery don’t necessarily start crying about it. When someone is being made fun of, the group dynamic pressures them: “You’re not going to cry now, are you? Come on, it’s just jokes.” And the person responds, “No, no, it’s okay, I can handle this.” That’s what they say out loud: “I can handle it, I’ve got this.”

But on the Day of Judgment, that same person who said “I got this” - actually, when they said “I got this,” they didn’t really have it. The pain was real, the humiliation was real, and the consequences will be real.

وَلَا تَلْمِزُوٓا۟ أَنفُسَكُمْ - and don’t insult yourselves. Notice: when you insult another believer, Allah ﷻ says you’re insulting yourself. We’re all one body, so attacking another Muslim is self-harm.

وَلَا تَنَابَزُوا۟ بِٱلْأَلْقَـٰبِ - and don’t call each other by offensive nicknames. Don’t label people with names meant to demean them.

بِئْسَ ٱلِٱسْمُ ٱلْفُسُوقُ بَعْدَ ٱلْإِيمَـٰنِ - how terrible is the name of corruption after having attained faith. You’ve been honored with iman, and now you want to be known for mockery and meanness?

وَمَن لَّمْ يَتُبْ فَأُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ هُمُ ٱلظَّـٰلِمُونَ - and whoever does not repent from this behavior, those are the true wrongdoers. This isn’t a small matter. Allah ﷻ categorizes those who persist in mocking others as oppressors - wrongdoers who harm both themselves and their community.

Ayah 11–13 · linked video: 03. Al-Hujurat (Ayah 11-13)

Surah Al-Hujurat: Ayahs 11-13

Ayah 11

يَـٰٓأَيُّهَا ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ لَا يَسْخَرْ قَوْمٌ مِّن قَوْمٍ عَسَىٰٓ أَن يَكُونُوا۟ خَيْرًا مِّنْهُمْ وَلَا نِسَآءٌ مِّن نِّسَآءٍ عَسَىٰٓ أَن يَكُنَّ خَيْرًا مِّنْهُنَّ ۖ وَلَا تَلْمِزُوٓا۟ أَنفُسَكُمْ وَلَا تَنَابَزُوا۟ بِٱلْأَلْقَـٰبِ ۖ بِئْسَ ٱلِٱسْمُ ٱلْفُسُوقُ بَعْدَ ٱلْإِيمَـٰنِ ۚ وَمَن لَّمْ يَتُبْ فَأُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ هُمُ ٱلظَّـٰلِمُونَ

The Prohibition Against Mockery

Allah ﷻ addresses the believers directly with a powerful prohibition: groups of men should not mock other groups of men, and women should not mock other women. The divine wisdom behind this command is profound—perhaps those being mocked are actually better in the sight of Allah ﷻ than those doing the mocking. This is an ominous warning that should make us pause before we ever ridicule another person.

The Prophet ﷺ demonstrated the gravity of this prohibition through his reactions to seemingly minor incidents. There was a companion who had very thin legs, and when some of the other companions made light comments about his appearance while he was climbing a tree, the Prophet ﷺ became visibly upset. He made it clear that those thin legs carried more weight in the sight of Allah ﷻ than their mockery suggested. In another instance, a companion who could barely grow a beard—managing only a single hair—was cherished by the Prophet ﷺ, who declared that even that one hair outweighed the mountain of Uhud on the Day of Judgment.

This prohibition strikes at the heart of much modern entertainment. Consider how prevalent mockery has become in our culture—comedy routines built on ridiculing accents, physical appearances, gender differences, cultural practices, and political figures. We’ve normalized what Allah ﷻ has explicitly forbidden.

Gender-Specific Prohibitions

Allah ﷻ wisely separates the prohibition for men and women because mockery manifests differently across genders. Men may mock each other more directly, while women often employ subtle, nonverbal cues—a particular look, a raised eyebrow, a knowing glance exchanged between friends. Allah ﷻ sees all of it. You can mock someone without saying a single word, and that too falls under this prohibition.

The bullying that occurs in schools, particularly among young women, is a direct violation of this command. When groups of girls target one individual, commenting on her body, clothes, hair, or any other aspect of her appearance or personality, they’re engaging in precisely what this ayah forbids. The consequences of such behavior are devastating—some victims have taken their own lives as a result of relentless bullying.

The Meaning of Lamz

The phrase وَلَا تَلْمِزُوٓا۟ أَنفُسَكُمْ introduces us to the concept of لَمْز —finding and exposing someone’s flaws, whether real or imagined, using them to diminish that person, to be condescending, to destroy their confidence. This includes hurtful statements like “You’re never going to amount to anything” or any words that tear down someone’s sense of self-worth.

Remarkably, Allah ﷻ says “do not fault yourselves” rather than “do not fault each other.” This carries multiple meanings. First, as believers, we are one body—when you hurt another Muslim, you hurt yourself. Second, this prohibition extends even to how we speak about ourselves. Don’t engage in self-deprecation, constantly saying “I’m so dumb” or “I wish I was different.” Have respect for yourself, for Allah ﷻ created you with purpose and dignity.

The Prohibition of Name-Calling

The command وَلَا تَنَابَزُوا۟ بِٱلْأَلْقَـٰبِ addresses the practice of calling each other by hurtful nicknames. The word نَبَز typically refers to bad names, while لَقَب can refer to any kind of nickname. By combining both terms, Allah ﷻ covers the full spectrum—including sarcasm. Someone might use a positive word like “genius” but deliver it sarcastically, turning a compliment into an insult. This too is forbidden.

The distinction between acceptable and unacceptable nicknames lies in consent and respect. If someone genuinely likes a nickname and it’s used affectionately, that may be permissible. However, if someone dislikes being called by a particular name, it’s your obligation to stop. It’s not unreasonable for someone to say, “I don’t appreciate that nickname. Please use my actual name.”

The Importance of Respectful Titles

This principle extends to the appropriate use of titles based on age, position, and relationship. If you’re sixteen years old, it’s not appropriate to address someone in their thirties as “bro.” There’s a hierarchy of respect that Islam encourages us to maintain. Younger people should address their elders with appropriate titles—calling teachers “Ustadh,” addressing scholars with respect, using culturally appropriate honorifics.

This isn’t about rigid formality for its own sake; it’s about maintaining boundaries that preserve the dignity of relationships. When respect breaks down, other aspects of the relationship begin to deteriorate as well. Even among peers, there are appropriate ways to address those who hold positions of responsibility—an imam, a teacher, an employer who is older than you.

Cultural norms play a significant role here. In some cultures, adding respectful suffixes to names is standard practice. If you have employees or colleagues who are older than you, addressing them with appropriate respect isn’t just courtesy—it’s part of maintaining healthy social bonds.

The Connection Between Speech and Faith

Allah ﷻ makes a stunning declaration: بِئْسَ ٱلِٱسْمُ ٱلْفُسُوقُ بَعْدَ ٱلْإِيمَـٰنِ —“Wretched is the name of disobedience after faith.” The phrase الِٱسْمُ ٱلْفُسُوقُ refers to “the corrupt noun”—profanity, vulgar language, obscene words. Allah ﷻ is saying that such speech is fundamentally incompatible with iman.

Every container gives out what it contains. If there’s iman in your heart, good words emerge. If there’s no iman, filthy words pour out. The state of your tongue reveals the state of your heart. If you find yourself regularly using profanity, dropping four-letter words casually in conversation, there’s an iman problem that needs to be addressed. You can’t claim the mantle of faith while your speech contradicts it.

This principle even extends to disguised profanity—those substitute words everyone knows are meant to replace actual curse words. The intent is the same, and Allah ﷻ knows what you meant.

Even words like “stupid,” “dumb,” or “pathetic” lower the bar of respectful speech. Once you drop that standard, it becomes easier to descend further. These words used by people who lack the intelligence or vocabulary to express their frustration constructively. Profanity is the bark of someone who can’t articulate themselves any other way.

The Call to Repentance

The ayah concludes with a stark warning: وَمَن لَّمْ يَتُبْ فَأُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ هُمُ ٱلظَّـٰلِمُونَ—“And whoever does not repent, then it is those who are the wrongdoers.” The door to repentance remains open, but those who refuse to change, who continue in these prohibited behaviors, place themselves among the wrongdoers. This is serious—it’s not just bad manners being discussed here, but a matter of spiritual consequence.

Ayah 12

يَـٰٓأَيُّهَا ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ ٱجْتَنِبُوا۟ كَثِيرًا مِّنَ ٱلظَّنِّ إِنَّ بَعْضَ ٱلظَّنِّ إِثْمٌ ۖ وَلَا تَجَسَّسُوا۟ وَلَا يَغْتَب بَّعْضُكُم بَعْضًا ۚ أَيُحِبُّ أَحَدُكُمْ أَن يَأْكُلَ لَحْمَ أَخِيهِ مَيْتًا فَكَرِهْتُمُوهُ ۚ وَٱتَّقُوا۟ ٱللَّهَ ۚ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ تَوَّابٌ رَّحِيمٌ

Avoiding Negative Assumptions

Allah ﷻ commands the believers: ٱجْتَنِبُوا۟ كَثِيرًا مِّنَ ٱلظَّنِّ —“Avoid much assumption.” The word ظَنّ refers to assumptions, particularly negative ones. When you see two people walking together, don’t immediately assume something inappropriate. If they appear to be married, accept that at face value and move on with your business.

Give people the benefit of the doubt. Someone takes a phone call and steps into another room—don’t assume they’re hiding something sinister. A husband asks his wife, “How was your day?”—don’t read malicious intent into a simple question. It’s remarkable how quickly we can twist innocent words into imagined insults, especially between spouses. “So how was your day?” “So? What do you mean ‘so’? Are you implying I didn’t do anything today?”

The term ظَنّ is particularly significant because it indicates confident assumption—you’re pretty sure someone means badly, you’re convinced there’s ill intent in their heart. Allah ﷻ says إِنَّ بَعْضَ ٱلظَّنِّ إِثْمٌ —“Indeed, some assumption is sin.” The only destination that kind of thinking leads to is sin itself.

The Prohibition of Spying

وَلَا تَجَسَّسُوا۟ —“And do not spy.” Don’t eavesdrop on others’ conversations. Don’t read other people’s private messages. Someone leaves their laptop open with their email visible—don’t look. Someone’s phone receives a text message—don’t read it. These are their private affairs.

This prohibition extends to all forms of snooping. Standing nearby when someone is on the phone so you can overhear their conversation—that’s spying. Having a conversation with one person while your attention is focused on listening to another conversation happening nearby—that’s prohibited. Walking into someone’s house and noticing the neighbor’s lights are on and window is open—don’t look in. Don’t investigate what’s happening in other people’s spaces.

You’re at a hotel and notice some doors are open along the hallway—keep walking. Don’t peer into other people’s rooms out of curiosity. We acquire sin so easily through these seemingly small actions, yet each one is a violation of the boundaries Allah ﷻ has established.

The prohibition even extends to following people to discover their activities. “I know he’s up to something, so I followed him.” This is not permissible. Even if you discover something true, the method by which you discovered it is sinful.

The Gravity of Backbiting

وَلَا يَغْتَب بَّعْضُكُم بَعْضًا —“And do not backbite each other.” It’s critical to understand that غِيبَة —backbiting—is not lying. That’s a separate sin called نَمِيمَة . Backbiting is speaking truth about someone in their absence in a way that they would dislike.

“You know that brother? He has this problem.” Even if it’s true, if you’re sharing it with others, it’s backbiting. There is a confidentiality that exists between believers, just as there’s doctor-patient confidentiality. If you stumble upon someone’s secret, you’re obligated to keep it. You cannot share it with anyone. Exposing people’s private matters to others is a form of betrayal.

The Powerful Analogy

To illustrate how revolting this practice is, Allah ﷻ presents a visceral image: أَيُحِبُّ أَحَدُكُمْ أَن يَأْكُلَ لَحْمَ أَخِيهِ مَيْتًا —“Would one of you like to eat the flesh of his brother when dead?”

Imagine it—your brother lying there dead, and you’re being asked to consume his flesh. فَكَرِهْتُمُوهُ —you’re disgusted by the very thought. You find it repulsive. Allah ﷻ says that’s exactly how disgusted you should be by backbiting. That’s the level of revulsion that should fill you when you hear someone speaking about another person behind their back.

This means that when you hear backbiting, you should leave the conversation. If someone begins to share private details about another person—“I went to their house and saw how they live” or “They told me something in confidence”—stop them. When someone invites you into their home, they’re trusting you. You cannot violate that trust by discussing what you observed or what they shared with you.

Divine Mercy and the Door to Repentance

The ayah closes with hope: وَٱتَّقُوا۟ ٱللَّهَ ۚ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ تَوَّابٌ رَّحِيمٌ —“And fear Allah; indeed, Allah is Accepting of repentance and Merciful.”

After listing these serious prohibitions, Allah ﷻ reminds us of His nature. He is تَوَّاب —continually accepting of repentance, always ready to forgive those who turn back to Him. He is رَّحِيم —perpetually merciful and loving. No matter how deeply we’ve fallen into these patterns of behavior, the door to return to Allah ﷻ remains open.

Ayah 13

يَـٰٓأَيُّهَا ٱلنَّاسُ إِنَّا خَلَقْنَـٰكُم مِّن ذَكَرٍ وَأُنثَىٰ وَجَعَلْنَـٰكُمْ شُعُوبًا وَقَبَآئِلَ لِتَعَارَفُوٓا۟ ۚ إِنَّ أَكْرَمَكُمْ عِندَ ٱللَّهِ أَتْقَىٰكُمْ ۚ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ عَلِيمٌ خَبِيرٌ

The Universal Address

The address shifts dramatically: يَـٰٓأَيُّهَا ٱلنَّاسُ —“O humanity.” No longer is Allah ﷻ addressing only the believers; now He speaks to all of mankind. This is a universal principle that transcends religious boundaries.

إِنَّا خَلَقْنَـٰكُم مِّن ذَكَرٍ وَأُنثَىٰ —“We have created all of you from a male and a female.” This simple statement establishes a profound truth: we are one family. Every human being shares the same origin, the same parents in Adam (AS) and Hawwa. No person is inherently better than another. No nation holds superiority over another. No race is more valuable in the sight of Allah ﷻ than any other.

The Purpose of Diversity

وَجَعَلْنَـٰكُمْ شُعُوبًا وَقَبَآئِلَ —“And We made you peoples and tribes.” The word شُعُوب refers to different populations and peoples, while قَبَآئِل refers to tribes and smaller groupings. Allah ﷻ is the One who created this diversity.

Why? لِتَعَارَفُوٓا۟ —“So that you may know one another.” The root ع-ر-ف relates to recognition and knowledge. Allah ﷻ created cultural diversity so that we can recognize each other, understand different customs, appreciate various traditions. Just as we marvel at different species of flowers, varieties of landscapes, types of climates and waters, we should appreciate the beautiful diversity of human cultures.

This is the divine purpose behind human variety—not so that we can feel superior to one another, not so we can mock different customs or ridicule unfamiliar traditions, but so we can enjoy and learn from the rich tapestry of human experience. We’re meant to respect one another, appreciate our differences, and find beauty in the diversity that Allah ﷻ has woven into His creation.

The True Standard of Honor

إِنَّ أَكْرَمَكُمْ عِندَ ٱللَّهِ أَتْقَىٰكُمْ —“Indeed, the most noble of you in the sight of Allah is the most righteous of you.”

After establishing our common origin and the purpose of our diversity, Allah ﷻ reveals the only measure of superiority that matters: تَقْوَىٰ . This word encompasses God-consciousness, protective awareness of Allah ﷻ, and the careful guarding of oneself from sin.

Your value in the sight of Allah ﷻ has nothing to do with your ethnicity, your tribe, your nation, your wealth, your appearance, or your lineage. The only thing that elevates one person above another is how carefully they guard themselves from displeasing Allah ﷻ, how conscious they are of His presence, how diligently they strive to live according to His guidance.

Divine Knowledge

إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ عَلِيمٌ خَبِيرٌ —“Indeed, Allah is Knowing and Acquainted.”

The ayah concludes by reminding us that Allah ﷻ is عَلِيم —possessed of complete knowledge. He knows everything about everyone. He is also خَبِير —intimately aware, completely informed of all details. He knows who truly has taqwa and who merely pretends. He sees beyond external appearances and social status to the reality of the heart.

We cannot fool Allah ﷻ with outward displays while our hearts are corrupt. We cannot claim superiority based on superficial characteristics when He judges by the only standard that truly matters—the state of our relationship with Him.

Ayah 13–18 · linked video: 04. Al-Hujurat (Ayah 13-18)

Surah Al-Hujurat: Commentary on Ayahs 13-18

Ayah 13

يَـٰٓأَيُّهَا ٱلنَّاسُ إِنَّا خَلَقْنَـٰكُم مِّن ذَكَرٍ وَأُنثَىٰ وَجَعَلْنَـٰكُمْ شُعُوبًا وَقَبَآئِلَ لِتَعَارَفُوٓا۟ ۚ إِنَّ أَكْرَمَكُمْ عِندَ ٱللَّهِ أَتْقَىٰكُمْ ۚ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ عَلِيمٌ خَبِيرٌ

This ayah establishes a fundamental principle that reshapes how we understand human dignity and worth. When Allah ﷻ declares that the most honored among you in His sight is the one with the most taqwa, He dismantles all systems of tribal, racial and ethnic superiority in a single statement.

The beauty of this criterion is that taqwa resides in the heart, completely invisible to human eyes. I cannot measure anyone’s taqwa, which means I can never claim to know who deserves more respect than others. This is precisely why Allah ﷻ says elsewhere in the Quran, “We have honored all the Children of Adam” (17:70). The honor is inherent to our humanity itself, not tied to our ancestry or appearance.

Race, ethnicity and tribal affiliation are no longer issues that determine worth. They become merely means of identification and recognition, not hierarchy. The ayah concludes with Allah’s Names العليم (the All-Knowing) and خبير (the All-Aware), reminding us that while we cannot judge what lies in hearts, He ﷻ knows and sees everything perfectly.

Ayah 14

۞ قَالَتِ ٱلْأَعْرَابُ ءَامَنَّا ۖ قُل لَّمْ تُؤْمِنُوا۟ وَلَـٰكِن قُولُوٓا۟ أَسْلَمْنَا وَلَمَّا يَدْخُلِ ٱلْإِيمَـٰنُ فِى قُلُوبِكُمْ ۖ وَإِن تُطِيعُوا۟ ٱللَّهَ وَرَسُولَهُۥ لَا يَلِتْكُم مِّنْ أَعْمَـٰلِكُمْ شَيْـًٔا ۚ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ غَفُورٌ رَّحِيمٌ

The Bedouins mentioned earlier in the surah who were casual in their approach to the Prophet ﷺ now claim, “We have iman, we’re Muslims. What else is there?” They see their outward compliance with Islamic practices as the complete picture. Allah ﷻ corrects this misunderstanding directly: “You have not yet believed. Rather, say ‘We have submitted (aslamna).’”

The modern equivalent of this attitude exists today. Someone might say, “I know how to pray. I go to Jumu’ah. I’ve memorized Quran. I’m Muslim just like anyone else. I don’t need to learn more. I already have iman.” But Allah ﷻ is teaching us that iman is deeper than outward actions alone.

The crucial phrase comes next: “Faith has not yet entered your hearts.” This isn’t a condemnation but rather an acknowledgment that iman is a journey, a work in progress. Earlier in this surah, Allah ﷻ described true iman as something that increases when His ayahs are recited (49:15). Real iman manifests in the heart through love of the Prophet ﷺ, recognition of his elevated status, and restraint of the tongue from speaking inappropriately.

The ayah offers encouragement: “If you obey Allah and His Messenger, He will not diminish your deeds in any way.” Your actions still count, your Islam is valid, but understand that there are deeper levels of faith to pursue. The closing with Allah’s Names الغفور الرحيم (the Extremely Forgiving, the Always Merciful) emphasizes that this journey toward deeper iman is one He ﷻ supports with His forgiveness and mercy.

Understanding Islam versus Iman

This distinction between Islam and iman is essential for maintaining a healthy Muslim community. Consider a father with two sons: one has memorized the Quran and thousands of hadith, teaches Islam, prays consistently and performs Hajj regularly. The other ran away from home, struggled with substance abuse, and now runs a business involving prohibited transactions. Both are his sons. Both are Muslim.

When their father passes away and the inheritance must be distributed according to Allah’s command, do we give a larger share to the righteous son and a smaller share to the wayward one? No. Because we can only judge based on Islam, not iman. We cannot see into hearts to measure faith.

How do we determine if someone has Islam? Their claim is sufficient. If someone says “As-salamu alaykum” to you, the Quran explicitly forbids you from saying they are not a believer (4:94). You don’t get to explore whether they have iman or not. They don’t get to judge whether you have iman. It is enough that we recognize each other’s Islam.

This principle prevents catastrophic division within the Muslim community. When Muslims fought each other, the Quran still addressed both groups as believers: “If two groups of believers fight…” (49:9). Even when Allah ﷻ addresses those whose actions contradict their words, He says, “O you who believe, why do you say what you do not do?” (61:2). He doesn’t call them hypocrites, even though saying one thing and doing another is a characteristic of hypocrisy.

Why is this so important? Because when the Quran commands us to struggle against the disbelievers and hypocrites, if we had the authority to label other Muslims as hypocrites, we would have license to fight them. Civil society among Muslims would collapse. There would be constant internal warfare based on people’s judgments of each other’s hearts.

Stay away from those who casually label other Muslims as hypocrites. This practice tears apart the fabric of the community. Yes, we can and must judge actions as right or wrong. If someone steals, the act of stealing is wrong. If someone commits adultery, that action is a grave evil. But we never get to judge whether that person has iman or not.

Remember the woman who was stoned during the Prophet’s time. Her tawbah was so sincere that the Prophet ﷺ said her repentance was enough to forgive the entire city of Madinah. She committed a major sin, but her iman and repentance were between her and Allah ﷻ. We are never in a position to make such judgments.

When you disagree with someone’s understanding of faith, their interpretation, their point of view or school of thought, there is a civil way to engage. The way you speak to someone while assuming they have iman will be fundamentally different from how you’d speak while thinking they are inherently corrupt. The tone, the anger, the rage you carry into such conversations creates more division and pain in the community.

Civil conversation between Muslims must be rooted in the assumption that the other person has iman. This is not just about being polite; it’s about recognizing the limits Allah ﷻ has placed on our judgment.

Ayah 15

إِنَّمَا ٱلْمُؤْمِنُونَ ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ بِٱللَّهِ وَرَسُولِهِۦ ثُمَّ لَمْ يَرْتَابُوا۟ وَجَـٰهَدُوا۟ بِأَمْوَٰلِهِمْ وَأَنفُسِهِمْ فِى سَبِيلِ ٱللَّهِ ۚ أُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ هُمُ ٱلصَّـٰدِقُونَ

After establishing what Islam looks like outwardly, Allah ﷻ now describes the characteristics of true believers. This is the third time in the Quran that the word يَرْتَابُوا (yartabu - to fall into doubt) appears. It appeared twice in Surah Al-Anfal (8:2), and now appears twice in this passage.

True believers are defined by three qualities: First, they have believed in Allah ﷻ and His Messenger ﷺ. Second, they do not fall into doubt whatsoever about this belief. Third, they struggle with their wealth and their very selves in the path of Allah ﷻ.

Let’s examine this third characteristic more carefully. The phrase “in the path of Allah” can also be understood as “in the mission of Allah” or “in the mission that Allah gave His Prophet.” The true believer doesn’t see religion as merely a set of rituals to complete. They see it as a cause, a mission to commit to. They understand that their wealth and their efforts should be directed toward advancing this mission.

The ayah concludes: “Those are the ones who are truthful.” After the Bedouins claimed to have iman, Allah ﷻ now clarifies who truly possesses what they claimed. The title of “truthful” belongs to those whose hearts are free from doubt and whose resources are actively engaged in Allah’s cause.

Ayah 16

قُلْ أَتُعَلِّمُونَ ٱللَّهَ بِدِينِكُمْ وَٱللَّهُ يَعْلَمُ مَا فِى ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَمَا فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ ۚ وَٱللَّهُ بِكُلِّ شَىْءٍ عَلِيمٌ

The Bedouins claimed to have iman when they did not. Now Allah ﷻ poses a rhetorical question to expose the absurdity of their presumption: “Are you going to teach Allah about your religion?”

This ayah addresses a problem that extends beyond those original Bedouins. There are people today who develop entire philosophies about what Islam represents without ever grounding their ideas in the Quran or authentic hadith. They speak about Islam wanting “peace” and “justice” but define these terms according to their own understanding rather than Allah’s revelation. When someone writes a book about Islam that has nothing to do with Allah’s Book itself, they are essentially claiming to teach Allah ﷻ about religion.

Allah ﷻ knows what is in the heavens and what is in the earth. He ﷻ is All-Knowing of everything. The contrast is stark: humans with limited knowledge and perspective presume to define religion independently of divine revelation, while Allah ﷻ possesses complete and perfect knowledge of all things.

Ayah 17

يَمُنُّونَ عَلَيْكَ أَنْ أَسْلَمُوا۟ ۖ قُل لَّا تَمُنُّونَ عَلَىَّ إِسْلَـٰمَكُم ۖ بَلِ ٱللَّهُ يَمُنُّ عَلَيْكُمْ أَنْ هَدَىٰكُمْ لِلْإِيمَـٰنِ إِن كُنتُمْ صَـٰدِقِينَ

The Bedouins approached the Prophet ﷺ with an attitude of conferring a favor: “Look what we’ve done for you. We’ve become Muslim. We’re adding to your numbers, aren’t we? You were so few in Makkah, and now you have us.”

The Arabic word يَمُنُّونَ (yamunnuna) means to impose one’s favor upon someone, to constantly remind them of what you’ve done for them. A Muslim begins to act as though they are doing Islam a favor by being part of it. Allah ﷻ corrects this backwards understanding.

“Tell them: Don’t impose your Islam as a favor upon me.” The reality is that we need Islam infinitely more than Islam needs us. Teachers of Quran will come and go. Teachers of Arabic will come and go. Scholars will come and go. But the Quran will remain. Arabic will remain. Fiqh will remain. We are all dispensable. None of us are irreplaceable.

You might think that the masjid couldn’t stand on its feet without you, that the pillars would collapse if you weren’t the treasurer or the volunteer coordinator. But you will be gone one day, and after people grieve for a few weeks, life will move on. This is true for all of us without exception.

If the world moved forward after the Prophet ﷺ departed, then it will certainly move forward when you and I are gone. We are not doing a favor to Islam. We are not serving Islam out of benevolence. Islam is serving us.

“Rather, Allah confers His favor upon you that He guided you to true faith, if indeed you are truthful.” Every moment of guidance, every step toward sincere iman, every understanding we gain is Allah’s favor to us. We owe Him ﷻ everything. He owes us nothing.

Ayah 18

إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ يَعْلَمُ غَيْبَ ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَٱلْأَرْضِ ۚ وَٱللَّهُ بَصِيرٌۢ بِمَا تَعْمَلُونَ

The surah concludes with a powerful reminder of Allah’s complete knowledge. He ﷻ knows the unseen of the heavens and the earth. Nothing is hidden from Him. The word غَيْبَ (ghayb - the unseen) encompasses everything beyond our perception, including what lies in human hearts.

The final phrase shifts to sight: “And Allah is All-Seeing of what you do.” He ﷻ not only knows the unseen realm but also sees every action we perform in the visible world. Our words, our deeds, our choices - all are in full view before Him.

This closing serves as both a warning and a comfort. It warns against hypocrisy and pretense, reminding us that no performance of faith can fool Allah ﷻ. But it also comforts the sincere believer who struggles with their iman, knowing that Allah ﷻ sees their efforts and their intentions, even when other people cannot.

Surah Al-Hujurat has taken us on a journey through the foundations of a civilized Muslim community: proper conduct toward the Prophet ﷺ, verification before spreading news, reconciliation between believers, respect for each other’s dignity, the universal equality of all humans, and finally, an honest understanding of what Islam and iman truly mean. May Allah ﷻ make us among those who live up to these beautiful teachings.
